This code reshapes and aggregates the nitrogen-based pollutant data for comparison.
It selects NO, NO$_2$, and NOx along with the site and datetime, then converts the data from wide to long format, creating a `pollutant` column and a `value` column.
The data are grouped by site, pollutant, year, month, and monthly mean values are calculated to reduce noise from hourly measurements.
Finally, a mid-month date is generated for plotting.
This process produces a tidy, long-format dataset that is ideal for creating comparative time-series plots of multiple pollutants across sites.

```{r, echo=FALSE, message=FALSE, warning= FALSE, dev="png", dpi=300, fig.width=10, fig.height=6, fig.pos='H'}
ggplot(no_compare, aes(x = date, y = value_mean, colour = pollutant)) +
  geom_line(size = 1.2) +
  geom_point(size = 1.2, alpha = 0.6) +
  facet_wrap(~ location, scales = "free_y") +
  scale_x_date(date_labels = "%b-%Y", date_breaks = "6 months") +
  scale_colour_manual(values = c("nitric_oxide" = "#1f78b4",
                                 "nitrogen_dioxide" = "#33a02c",
                                 "nitrogen_oxides_as_nitrogen_dioxide" = "#e31a1c"),
                      labels = c("NO", "NO2", "NOx")) +
  labs(
    title = "Comparative Time Series: NO, NO2, and NOx in Plymouth (2020–2025)",
    subtitle = "Traffic (Tavistock Road) vs Urban Background (Plymouth Centre)",
    x = "Date",
    y = "Concentration (µg/m³)",
    colour = "Pollutant"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 14),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 10),
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1),
    legend.position = "top",
    legend.title = element_text(face = "bold")
  )
```

The graph illustrates the time series of air pollutants—Nitric Oxide (NO), Nitrogen Dioxide (NO$_2$), and Nitrogen Oxides as NO$_2$ (NOx)—in Plymouth from April 2020 to April 2025, comparing two monitoring sites: Plymouth Centre (urban background) and Tavistock Road (traffic-related pollution).

At Tavistock Road, NO concentrations are consistently higher, correlating strongly with traffic emissions.
The observed peaks align with increased vehicular activity, indicating the need for effective traffic management strategies.
A downward trend in NO levels suggests potential improvements in air quality, possibly due to regulatory efforts.

The NOx measurements mirror the trends of NO and NO$_2$, highlighting the significant impact of traffic on urban air quality.
Overall, the data points to substantial differences in pollution levels between the two sites and emphasizes the importance of continued monitoring and targeted interventions to enhance air quality in Plymouth.

## (D) Sliding-Window Correlation Analysis of Pollutants

To explore how relationships between pollutants change over time, we applied a 30-day sliding window to daily-averaged concentrations of NO, NO$_2$, PM$_{2.5}$, and O$_3$.

```{r, echo=FALSE, message=FALSE, warning= FALSE}
library(tidyverse)
library(lubridate)
library(slider) 

# Selecting pollutants of interest
selected_pol <- c("NO", "NO2", "PM2.5", "O3")

# Filter and reshape data to wide format keeping only selected pollutants
cor_data <- air_long %>%
  filter(pollutant_short %in% selected_pol) %>%
  group_by(date, pollutant_short) %>%
  summarise(value = mean(value, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop") %>%
  pivot_wider(names_from = pollutant_short, values_from = value) # each pollutant has its own column (wide format)

# order data by date 
cor_data <- cor_data %>%
  arrange(date)

# Define sliding window size
# 30-day window, approximately one month
window_size <- 30

# Apply sliding window correlation
# - For each window, correlation matrix of selected pollutants is calculated
cor_results <- slider::slide(
  .x = cor_data,
  .f = ~ cor(.x %>% select(-date), use = "pairwise.complete.obs"),
  .before = window_size,
  .complete = TRUE
)

# Correlation matrices is combined with corresponding dates 
cor_df <- tibble(
  date = cor_data$date,
  cor_matrix = cor_results
)

# Correlation matrices are converted into tidy long format 
tidy_cor <- cor_df %>%
  filter(!map_lgl(cor_matrix, is.null)) %>%
  mutate(
    cor_table = purrr::map(cor_matrix, ~ {
      cm <- as.data.frame(.x)
      cm$var1 <- rownames(cm)
      cm %>%
        pivot_longer(cols = -var1, names_to = "var2", values_to = "correlation")
    })
  ) %>%
  select(date, cor_table) %>%
  unnest(cor_table) %>%
  filter(var1 != var2) %>%   # remove self-correlation
  filter(var1 < var2)        # keep only unique pairs
```

```{r, fig.align='center', echo=FALSE, message=FALSE, warning= FALSE}

# Heatmap of sliding correlations
library(ggplot2)
ggplot(tidy_cor, aes(x = date, y = interaction(var1, var2), fill = correlation)) +
  geom_tile() +
  scale_fill_gradient2(
    low = "blue", mid = "white", high = "red", midpoint = 0, limits = c(-1, 1)
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Sliding-Window Correlations Between Pollutants Over Time",
    x = "Date",
    y = "Pollutant Pair",
    fill = "Correlation"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    axis.text.y = element_text(size = 10),
    plot.title = element_text(size = 13.8, face = "bold")
  )
```

=\> The heatmap shows correlations for all pollutant pairs across the study period.
NO and NO$_2$ maintain a consistently strong positive correlation, reflecting their common sources from traffic and combustion and indicating a stable long-term relationship.
In contrast, PM$_{2.5}$ correlations with other pollutants fluctuate, sometimes even reversing, likely due to short-term pollution events or changing weather conditions.
A particularly interesting pattern is seen with NO$_2$ and O$_3$: their correlation shifts show clear reversals, highlighting seasonal photochemical processes.

For a closer look at how individual pollutants interact, we focus on the NO$_2$–O$_3$ pair.
The line plot below shows how their correlation changes over time, highlighting moments when the relationship strengthens, weakens, or even flips direction.
This detailed view complements the broader trends seen in the heatmap and helps us understand the dynamic interactions between these two important pollutants.

```{r, echo=FALSE, message=FALSE, warning= FALSE}
# A specific pollutant pair (NO2–O3) is selected and correlation is extracted for it  
pair <- "NO2.O3"
pair_cor <- tidy_cor %>%
  filter(interaction(var1, var2) == pair)
```

```{r, echo=FALSE, message=FALSE, warning= FALSE}
# Line plot for the selected pair showing how correlation changes over time
# Horizontal dashed line at y=0 to highlight positive/negative correlations
ggplot(pair_cor, aes(date, correlation)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed") +
  labs(
    title = paste("Correlation Over Time:", gsub("\\.", " – ", pair)),
    x = "Date",
    y = "Correlation"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()
```

=\> The line plot shows how the correlation between NO$_2$ and O$_3$ changes over time.
It starts slightly negative and later shifts to positive, reflecting periods when their interaction strengthens or weakens.
Overall, the relationship fluctuates around zero, highlighting that it is variable rather than stable.
